Pair students from Group C with A for peer coaching in the next independent practice."},"materials":{"optional":["Printed copies of the three incorrect solutions for students who need a visual reference","Timer set to 30–60 seconds for each vote round"],"required":["Board or projector with the three incorrect solutions written clearly","Student mini-whiteboards or paper for quick votes","Marker or pointer to highlight the exact sign in each line"]},"activitySteps":{"incorrectWorkedExample3":{"problem":"Solve the system: 2x + 3y = 10 and 4x + y = 10.","incorrectWork":"Multiply the second equation by −3: −3(4x + y = 10) → −12x + 3y = −30. ← sign flipped here (should be −12x − 3y = −30)
Add to the first: (2x + 3y) + (−12x + 3y) = 10 + (−30)
−10x + 6y = −20
Divide by 2: −5x + 3y = −10
From 4x + y = 10, y = 10 − 4x
Substitute: −5x + 3(10 − 4x) = −10
−5x + 30 − 12x = −10
−17x = −40
x = 40/17
y = 10 − 4(40/17) = 10/17
Final answer: (40/17, 10/17)."},"incorrectWorkedExamples":[{"problem":"Solve the system: 2x + y = 5 and x + 2y = 4.","incorrectWork":"From x + 2y = 4, isolate x: x = 4 − 2y.
Substitute into 2x + y = 5: 2(4 + 2y) + y = 5. ← sign flipped here (should be 2(4 − 2y) + y)
8 + 4y + y = 5
5y = −3
y = −3/5
x = 4 − 2(−3/5) = 4 + 6/5 = 26/5
Final answer: (26/5, −3/5)."},{"problem":"Solve the system: 4x + y = 9 and x − 2y = 1.","incorrectWork":"From x − 2y = 1, isolate x: x = 1 − 2y. ← sign flipped here (should be x = 1 + 2y)
Substitute into 4x + y = 9: 4(1 − 2y) + y = 9
4 − 8y + y = 9
−7y = 5
y = −5/7
x = 1 − 2(−5/7) = 1 + 10/7 = 17/7
Final answer: (17/7, −5/7)."},{"problem":"Solve the system: 2x + 3y = 10 and 4x + y = 10.","incorrectWork":"Multiply the second equation by −3: −3(4x + y = 10) → −12x + 3y = −30. ← sign flipped here (should be −12x − 3y = −30)
Add to the first: (2x + 3y) + (−12x + 3y) = 10 + (−30)
−10x + 6y = −20
Divide by 2: −5x + 3y = −10
From 4x + y = 10, y = 10 − 4x
Substitute: −5x + 3(10 − 4x) = −10
−5x + 30 − 12x = −10
−17x = −40
x = 40/17
y = 10 − 4(40/17) = 10/17
Final answer: (40/17, 10/17)."}],"incorrectWorkedExample1":{"problem":"Solve the system: 2x + y = 5 and x + 2y = 4.","incorrectWork":"From x + 2y = 4, isolate x: x = 4 − 2y.

Substitute into 4x + y = 9: 4(1 − 2y) + y = 9

6) −2x = 6 ⇒ x = −3
7) Substitute into 2x + 3y = 7: 2(−3) + 3y = 7 ⇒ −6 + 3y = 7

6) Solve for y: y = 4/3 − x
7) Substitute into 2x + 3y = 7: 2x + 3(4/3 − x) = 7

5) 14 + 3y = 1

gapGroups.title: Improper Fraction Handling
gapGroups.misconceptionSummary: Students manipulate coefficients written as fractions incorrectly when solving for a variable. The error produces wrong solutions because the reciprocal or appropriate operation is not applied correctly.
gapGroups.aiReasoning: PPQ Q3 and Q6 showed low accuracy (≈53.6%) with Q3 highConfWrongPct = 0.25, indicating many students answered confidently but incorrectly on fraction-equation items. The combination of error rates and confidence suggests a mixed conceptual/procedural gap rather than guessing.
successIndicators: Multiply both sides by the reciprocal and show work
Replace fractional coefficient with its reciprocal correctly
Check solution by substitution into original equation

targetObjective.description: Students manipulate coefficients written as fractions incorrectly when solving for a variable. The error produces wrong solutions because the reciprocal or appropriate operation is not applied correctly.

prerequisiteGaps.description: Solve linear equations in one variable.

impactedObjectives.description: Create equations and inequalities in one variable and use them to solve problems. Include equations arising from linear and quadratic functions, and simple rational and exponential functions.
Rearrange formulas to highlight a quantity of interest, using the same reasoning as in solving equations. *For example, rearrange Ohm's law $V = IR$ to highlight resistance $R$.*

evidence.mostCommonError: Dividing by numerator then denominator instead of multiplying by reciprocal
evidence.aiThinkingPattern: Treats division by a fraction as sequential division by numerator then denominator or fails to use reciprocal; likely a weak conceptual link between operations and fraction inversion.

wrongAnswerExplanations.explanation: The student likely miscalculated the operation needed to isolate the variable, possibly adding instead of multiplying by the reciprocal.
The student may have incorrectly simplified the equation, failing to apply the correct operation to the fraction coefficient.
The student might have confused the operation required to eliminate the fraction, leading to an incorrect manipulation of the equation.
The student may have incorrectly applied the fraction in the equation, possibly misunderstanding how to handle the coefficients.

correctAnswerSolution: Isolate the variable by moving all other terms to the opposite side of the equation.
If the variable has a coefficient that is a fraction, multiply both sides by the reciprocal of that fraction.
Simplify the resulting equation to solve for the variable.

moveOptions.title: Divide-by-Fraction Diagnosis
moveOptions.summary: Students analyze three incorrect solutions that divide by a fractional coefficient by splitting the division into two steps instead of using the reciprocal. The activity makes the single error visible and guides students to correct it and check by substitution.
moveOptions.targets: Solving one-step linear equations with fractional coefficients by multiplying both sides by the reciprocal; checking solutions by substitution
moveOptions.instructionalMove: Model the Incorrect Worked Example Analysis routine by projecting one incorrect student solution at a time. Prompt students to identify the exact step where the student divided by the fraction incorrectly and name the error. Guide students to produce the correct algebraic fix (multiply by the reciprocal) and verify by substitution. Repeat for each example and emphasize the link to rearranging formulas and later work with coefficients.
moveOptions.aiReasoning: Students are overgeneralizing division rules for fractional coefficients. The Incorrect Worked Example Analysis forces them to locate the single conceptual step (dividing by numerator then denominator) and compare its consequences to the reciprocal method. Whole-class guided analysis keeps setup minimal and allows the teacher to surface prerequisite gaps (meaning of a fractional coefficient, equality/balance reasoning) and connect the fix to downstream skills like rearranging formulas.

whatStudentsDo.label: Think
whatStudentsDo.detail: Read a projected student solution and decide which step applies the wrong rule for dividing by a fraction.

whatStudentsDo.label: Show
whatStudentsDo.detail: Write a quick answer on a personal whiteboard: 'Error step' and 'Correct next step' and hold it up on cue.

whatStudentsDo.label: Explain
whatStudentsDo.detail: Turn to a neighbor for 30 seconds and state why multiplying by the reciprocal isolates the variable.

whatYouDo.label: Project
whatYouDo.detail: Display one incorrect worked example at a time. Keep all arithmetic correct except for the single division-by-fraction error.

whatYouDo.label: Prompt
whatYouDo.detail: Ask students to point to the exact line with the error and to write the corrected algebraic step.

whatYouDo.label: Highlight
whatYouDo.detail: Show the correct reciprocal step on the board and substitute the solution back into the original equation for verification.

overview.importance: Analyzing only the division-by-fraction error helps students separate procedural confusion from other algebraic skills. The routine builds the habit of checking solutions and connects to rearranging formulas where multiplying by reciprocals appears again.

setup: Prepare a slide or board with the three incorrect worked examples exactly as written below.
Hand out mini whiteboards and markers to each student or instruct students to use paper.
Project the first example and set a visible timer for quick responses.
coreActivity: Project incorrect example 1. Ask students to quietly underline the line with the error.
Have students write on whiteboards: the line number with the error and one sentence naming the error rule.
Call for 3 volunteers to share where the error is and why that step is incorrect.
Write the correct step on the board: multiply both sides by the reciprocal of the coefficient (for example ×5/2) and solve.
Substitute the correct solution back into the original equation as a class check.
Repeat the routine for examples 2 and 3, asking students to compare the three errors and state the general rule: dividing by a/b means multiply by b/a.
discussionQuestions: Which specific step shows the misconception, and what single rule fixes it?
How does multiplying by the reciprocal keep the balance of the equation while splitting the divided-by-a-fraction method does not?
How will this rule help when you need to rearrange a formula like V = IR to solve for R?

incorrectWorkedExamples.incorrectWork:
(2/5)x = 7
x = 7 ÷ 2 ÷ 5  ← student treats division by 2/5 as dividing by 2 then by 5
7 ÷ 2 = 3.5
3.5 ÷ 5 = 0.7
x = 0.7

(3/4)x = 12
x = 12 ÷ 3 ÷ 4  ← student divides by numerator then denominator instead of using reciprocal
12 ÷ 3 = 4
4 ÷ 4 = 1
x = 1

(1/2)x = −8
x = −8 ÷ 1 ÷ 2  ← student splits division by 1/2 into ÷1 then ÷2 instead of ×2
−8 ÷ 1 = −8
−8 ÷ 2 = −4
x = −4

required: Projector or board with the three incorrect worked examples ready to display
One mini whiteboard and marker per student or paper and pencil
optional: Prepared slide showing the reciprocal as a visual (flip numerator and denominator) and a balance-scale sketch

groups.name: Group A: Needs Concrete Repair
groups.description: Students who wrote the wrong answer and cannot explain the reciprocal step. Focus on one additional concrete example and a balance model.

groups.name: Group B: Partial Understanding
groups.description: Students who identify the error but make arithmetic slips when fixing it. Focus on practicing the multiply-by-reciprocal step and substitution checks.

groups.name: Group C: Ready to Extend
groups.description: Students who correct all examples and explain why the reciprocal works. Offer a short challenge: rearrange V = IR to solve for R using the same reasoning.

studentGroupings.aiRecommendation: Use quick whiteboard responses and the first example’s responses to sort students into groups for a 5-minute follow-up. Prioritize Group A for concrete balance-model work and Group C for extension tasks.

moveOptions.title: Predict the Flip
moveOptions.summary: Students predict where the "divide by numerator then denominator" error will appear, then analyze two different incorrect solutions and explain why each gives the wrong final answer. The activity forces students to name the error, test the correct reciprocal move, and check by substitution.
moveOptions.targets: Recognize and correct division-by-fraction errors by multiplying both sides by the reciprocal; check solutions by substitution
moveOptions.instructionalMove: Guide the class through a Predict Misconceptions / Error Forecasting routine. Tell students the specific error pattern to watch for: treating ÷(a/b) as ÷a then ÷b. Have each half predict where that step will appear, analyze a different incorrect solution, and then explain why multiplying by the reciprocal fixes the error.
moveOptions.aiReasoning: Forecasting forces students to surface the exact operation-level misunderstanding before seeing the correct method. Splitting the class lets each group focus on a different incorrect example so the teacher can circulate and hear targeted reasoning. Comparing predictions to actual incorrect steps makes the structure of dividing by a fraction visible (reciprocal vs sequential division). This directly targets the weak conceptual link between 'divide by a fraction' and 'multiply by its reciprocal' and prepares students for rearranging formulas and checking by substitution.

whatStudentsDo.label: Predict
whatStudentsDo.detail: Look at each equation and say where a student might do ÷(a/b) as ÷a then ÷b. Write a one-sentence prediction for the error step.

whatStudentsDo.label: Analyze
whatStudentsDo.detail: Work in your half to read one full incorrect solution and mark the exact step that shows the error. Compute the wrong answer that follows from that step.

whatStudentsDo.label: Compare
whatStudentsDo.detail: Share findings with the other half and test the correct fix by multiplying both sides by the reciprocal and checking by substitution.

whatYouDo.label: Present
whatYouDo.detail: Project three incorrect solutions that each show the 'divide by numerator then denominator' error. Tell students which two examples each half will analyze.

whatYouDo.label: Circulate
whatYouDo.detail: Listen to each half’s prediction and reasoning. Ask one quick question to push for why reciprocal is the right structure.

whatYouDo.label: Model
whatYouDo.detail: After reconvening, show the correct reciprocal move and substitution check for one example. Emphasize the algebraic structure behind the step.

overview.importance: Predicting the error makes the faulty operation explicit before students fix it. Analyzing the wrong step then applying the reciprocal links procedural steps to the underlying structure and prepares students for rearranging formulas later.

setup: Project or write three incorrect solutions on the board (use the three examples provided).
Split the class into two halves by row or side of the room; assign each half one incorrect solution to analyze.
Give each student a quick prediction sheet or a blank line to write their predicted error step.
activitySteps.problem: Forecast where a divide-by-fraction error will occur, analyze the incorrect solution you are assigned, then show the correct reciprocal fix and check by substitution for these equations: (1) (1/3)x = 6, (2) (2/5)x = 15, (3) x/4 = 3/2.
coreActivity: Tell students to predict, in one sentence, the exact step where ÷(a/b) might be handled incorrectly for the three equations.
Split into two halves; assign each half one incorrect example to analyze and mark the single mistaken step.
Have each half compute the wrong final answer that follows from that mistaken step and write a one-line explanation of why that step is wrong.
Reconvene whole class. Ask a volunteer from each half to read their marked step and result aloud.
Model the correct move: multiply both sides by the reciprocal for one example and substitute the result back into the original equation to check.
Have students correct the other example themselves using the reciprocal and verify by substitution.
discussionQuestions: What single operation changed the result: treating ÷(a/b) as ÷a then ÷b, or multiplying by the reciprocal? Explain in one sentence.
How does multiplying both sides by the reciprocal keep the equation balanced? Show the substitution check.
Which downstream tasks (rearranging formulas, inverse operations) will still fail if you keep the 'divide then divide' habit?

incorrectWorkedExamples.incorrectWork:
(1/3)x = 6
x = 6 ÷ 1 ÷ 3  ← student treats ÷(1/3) as ÷1 then ÷3
x = 6 ÷ 1 = 6
x = 6 ÷ 3 = 2
Final: x = 2

required: Projected slide or whiteboard with the three incorrect solutions shown exactly as written above
optional: One-line prediction slips or mini whiteboards for students to write their predicted error step

groups.name: Group A: Predicted ÷(a/b) as ÷a then ÷b
groups.description: Students who answered items showing the specific error pattern. They focus on locating the mistaken step and computing the wrong result.

groups.name: Group B: Other strategies or partial checks
groups.description: Students who used a reciprocal or attempted a check but made an arithmetic slip or skipped substitution. They focus on verifying and explaining why the reciprocal works.

studentGroupings.aiRecommendation: Place students into halves based on the diagnostic item: one half contains students who produced the 'divide then divide' answers; the other half contains all other students. Keep groups stable for the 10-minute task so the teacher can circulate efficiently.

gapGroups.title: Switching Variables with Wrong Signs
gapGroups.misconceptionSummary: When solving systems, signs get swapped or assigned incorrectly during substitution or elimination. The result is solved values with signs reversed or paired with the wrong variable.
gapGroups.aiReasoning: PPQ Q4 accuracy was 50% with low highConfWrongPct (0.077), suggesting errors are more executional during algebraic manipulation than firmly held misconceptions. Many students make sign errors while combining equations or isolating variables.
successIndicators: Apply sign rules when adding or subtracting equations
Show each elimination/substitution step clearly
Verify solution by substituting into both equations

targetObjective.description: When solving systems, signs get swapped or assigned incorrectly during substitution or elimination. The result is solved values with signs reversed or paired with the wrong variable.

prerequisiteGaps.description: Solve word problems leading to equations of the form px + q = r and p(x + q) = r, where p, q, and r are specific rational numbers. Solve equations of these forms fluently. Compare an algebraic solution to an arithmetic solution, identifying the sequence of the operations used in each approach. *For example, the perimeter of a rectangle is 54 cm. Its length is 6 cm. What is its width?*

Understand solving an equation or inequality as a process of answering a question: which values from a specified set, if any, make the equation or inequality true? Use substitution to determine whether a given number in a specified set makes an equation or inequality true.

impactedObjectives.description: Solve systems of linear equations exactly and approximately (e.g., with graphs), focusing on pairs of linear equations in two variables.

Graph the solutions to a linear inequality in two variables as a half-plane (excluding the boundary in the case of a strict inequality), and graph the solution set to a system of linear inequalities in two variables as the intersection of the corresponding half-planes.

evidence.mostCommonError: Dropping or flipping signs during elimination or substitution
sampleStudentWork: x + y = 5; x − y = 1 => added to get 2x = 6 then wrote x = 1
Solved 3x − 2y = 4 by moving 2y as −2y -> sign error
evidence.aiThinkingPattern: Weak procedural attention to sign when combining terms; cognitive load during multi-step algebra causes execution errors.

wrongAnswerExplanations.explanation: The student likely miscalculated the substitution step, resulting in a sign error when isolating the variable.
The student may have incorrectly added or subtracted terms, leading to a wrong sign in the final answer.
The student might have confused the signs during elimination, resulting in a negative value for the variable that should have been positive.

correctAnswerSolution: Step 1: Isolate one variable in one of the equations.
Step 2: Substitute the isolated variable into the other equation.
Step 3: Solve for the remaining variable.
Step 4: Substitute back to find the value of the first variable.

moveOptions.title: Compare, Debate, and Correct Sign Errors
moveOptions.summary: Students analyze three student solutions that each contain a single sign error, debate which steps are wrong, and then compare solution strategies to avoid sign flips. The activity targets the habit of dropping or flipping signs during substitution and elimination.
moveOptions.targets: Maintain correct signs during substitution and elimination; show each elimination/substitution step; verify solutions by substitution into both equations
moveOptions.instructionalMove: Present three student solutions on the board using the Compare / Debate / Evaluate Strategies structure. Point to the single sign-flip error in each solution and ask students to identify the exact line where the sign changed incorrectly. After debate, model the correct method and show how substituting the result into both original equations catches the sign error.
moveOptions.aiReasoning: Comparing multiple flawed solutions makes the sign-flip pattern visible and forces students to name the exact operation that changed a sign. The Compare / Debate / Evaluate structure supports error-first instruction: students diagnose real mistaken steps before seeing the correct path. Whole-class debate reduces cognitive load by keeping the teacher in control while students practice checking sign rules and substitution, which directly builds algebraic thinking and the operations skills needed for later system-solving standards.

whatStudentsDo.label: Analyze
whatStudentsDo.detail: Read three complete student solutions and mark the single line in each where the sign was changed incorrectly.

whatStudentsDo.label: Vote
whatStudentsDo.detail: Show a quick signal or whiteboard answer to indicate which solution step they think is wrong.

whatStudentsDo.label: Debate
whatStudentsDo.detail: Explain aloud, in pairs or to the class, why that line is wrong and how to fix it.

whatYouDo.label: Display
whatYouDo.detail: Write the three incorrect student solutions clearly on the board and label them A, B, C.

whatYouDo.label: Prompt
whatYouDo.detail: Ask targeted questions that force students to point to the exact sign-error line and justify their choice.

whatYouDo.label: Model
whatYouDo.detail: After debate, solve the chosen system correctly step-by-step and substitute the solution into both equations to show verification.

overview.importance: The activity forces students to attend to the exact step where a sign was flipped. Verifying by substitution builds the habit that catches sign mistakes before the answer is finalized.

setup: Write three student solution write-ups (Examples A, B, C) on the board or a slide. Leave space below for notes.
Put one system on the side as the central problem students will solve correctly after the debate (use Example C system to connect analysis to correction).
activitySteps.problem: Solve the system: 2x + 3y = 10 and 4x + y = 10.
coreActivity: Show Examples A, B, C on the board and name the activity: Compare / Debate / Evaluate Strategies.
Ask students to write on a mini-whiteboard which line in each example contains the single sign error, then hold up the board to vote.
Call on students who voted differently to state their reasoning and point to the exact symbol that changed incorrectly.

List three solving strategies on the board for the central problem: (1) isolate x then substitute, (2) isolate y then substitute, (3) eliminate by multiplying and adding. Write each strategy as 2–3 short steps.
Lead a 3–4 minute whole-class debate: for each strategy ask, 'Where could a sign flip happen? How would you check?' and have volunteers defend which strategy reduces sign risk.

Model the correct solution for the central problem step-by-step. Substitute the final numbers into both original equations to verify and explicitly show how verification would catch the sign flips in A and B.
discussionQuestions: Which exact operation in each example caused the sign to flip? Point to the symbol and say why it is wrong.
When you multiply an equation by −3 or move a term across the equals sign, what quick check can you do to confirm signs are correct?
How does substituting your final (x,y) into both equations help catch a sign error before you declare the answer finished?

incorrectWorkedExamples.problem: Solve the system: 2x + y = 5 and x + 2y = 4.
incorrectWorkedExamples.incorrectWork:
From x + 2y = 4, isolate x: x = 4 − 2y.
Substitute into 2x + y = 5: 2(4 + 2y) + y = 5. ← sign flipped here (should be 2(4 − 2y) + y)
8 + 4y + y = 5
5y = −3
y = −3/5
x = 4 − 2(−3/5) = 4 + 6/5 = 26/5
Final answer: (26/5, −3/5).

incorrectWorkedExamples.problem: Solve the system: 4x + y = 9 and x − 2y = 1.
incorrectWorkedExamples.incorrectWork:
From x − 2y = 1, isolate x: x = 1 − 2y. ← sign flipped here (should be x = 1 + 2y)
Substitute into 4x + y = 9: 4(1 − 2y) + y = 9
4 − 8y + y = 9
−7y = 5
y = −5/7
x = 1 − 2(−5/7) = 1 + 10/7 = 17/7
Final answer: (17/7, −5/7).

Multiply the second equation by −3: −3(4x + y = 10) → −12x + 3y = −30. ← sign flipped here (should be −12x − 3y = −30)
Add to the first: (2x + 3y) + (−12x + 3y) = 10 + (−30)
−10x + 6y = −20
Divide by 2: −5x + 3y = −10
From 4x + y = 10, y = 10 − 4x
Substitute: −5x + 3(10 − 4x) = −10
−5x + 30 − 12x = −10
−17x = −40
x = 40/17
y = 10 − 4(40/17) = 10/17
Final answer: (40/17, 10/17).

required: Board or projector with the three incorrect solutions written clearly
Student mini-whiteboards or paper for quick votes
Marker or pointer to highlight the exact sign in each line
optional: Printed copies of the three incorrect solutions for students who need a visual reference
Timer set to 30–60 seconds for each vote round

groups.name: Group A: Needs Direct Sign Support
groups.description: Students who make frequent sign flips. Focus on identifying the sign-change step and verbalizing the rule for multiplying or moving terms.

groups.name: Group B: Partial Procedural Fluency
groups.description: Students who sometimes lose track of signs under load. Focus on comparing the three strategies and practicing quick verification by substitution.

groups.name: Group C: Ready to Explain
groups.description: Students who rarely flip signs. Focus on explaining why each incorrect step is wrong and coaching others during the debate.

studentGroupings.aiRecommendation: Use the whole-class activity for initial diagnosis. After the lesson, pull Groups A and B for short targeted practice where the teacher quickly models sign rules and students solve 1–2 similar systems with immediate substitution checks. Pair students from Group C with A for peer coaching in the next independent practice.

moveOptions.title: Math Hospital: Sign-Flip Diagnosis
moveOptions.summary: Students act as 'doctors' who diagnose and repair incorrect student work that contains a single sign swap during elimination or substitution. The activity forces attention to the exact line where a sign changed and ends with verification by substituting solutions into both original equations.
moveOptions.targets: Maintain correct signs when adding/subtracting equations and when distributing negatives during substitution; show each elimination/substitution step and verify solutions by substitution.
moveOptions.instructionalMove: Guide students through a Math Hospital / Diagnose & Repair routine. Assign each half of the class a different incorrect patient solution that contains the specific sign-swap error pattern. Prompt each group to mark the exact step where the sign was changed incorrectly, fix that line, recompute the solution, and verify both original equations. Circulate and ask students to show their corrected step and a substitution check.
moveOptions.aiReasoning: The Math Hospital structure forces students to pinpoint the single sign-flip error and justify a precise repair, which directly attacks the identified cognitive error (dropping or flipping signs during elimination/substitution). Splitting the class into two groups lets each group diagnose a different incorrect example in parallel so students see multiple manifestations of the same error without long whole-class time. Guided Error Analysis aligns with the error-first approach required by RightOn: students first analyze incorrect reasoning, then construct and verify corrections, which strengthens procedural attention to signs and supports later algebraic standards that rely on precise equation manipulation.

whatStudentsDo.label: Diagnose
whatStudentsDo.detail: Read an assigned incorrect student solution line by line and underline the single step where a sign was changed or dropped.

whatStudentsDo.label: Repair
whatStudentsDo.detail: Write a corrected version of the faulty line and recompute the remaining steps to produce a corrected solution.

whatStudentsDo.label: Verify
whatStudentsDo.detail: Substitute the corrected values back into both original equations and report whether the pair checks.

whatYouDo.label: Assign
whatYouDo.detail: Project the system and hand each half a different printed incorrect 'patient' solution to diagnose.

whatYouDo.label: Circulate
whatYouDo.detail: Visit each group, ask for the exact line that shows the sign swap, and prompt them to explain the algebraic consequence of that line.

whatYouDo.label: Debrief
whatYouDo.detail: Bring the class together, put the third incorrect patient on the board, and lead a 5-minute whole-class repair and verification.

overview.importance: The activity directly forces students to locate the sign-flip error instead of guessing where a mistake happened. Repairing and substituting immediately links procedural steps to the meaning of equations and prevents the same error from recurring.

setup: Project the system 2x + 3y = 7 and 4x − 3y = 1 on the board.
Print or display three incorrect worked examples (patients). Give Group A example 1 and Group B example 2. Keep example 3 for whole-class review.
Prepare two colored pens so students can mark the error line and the corrected line.
activitySteps.problem: Solve the system: 2x + 3y = 7 and 4x − 3y = 1. (Work shows one sign‐flip error in each student's solution.)
coreActivity: Tell students: you are doctors. Your patient is the incorrect solution and has exactly one sign error to repair.
Give Group A patient 1 and Group B patient 2. Instruct each group to find the exact line with the sign-flip and mark it.
Have each group write the corrected line in a different color and finish the arithmetic to find the corrected solution.
Require each group to test the corrected pair by substituting both values into both original equations and record the check.
Bring the class together and display patient 3. Ask one group to present their diagnosis and the other to compare the repair.
Lead a quick whole-class verification of patient 3, highlighting the relationship between distributing negatives, combining like terms, and substitution.
discussionQuestions: Exactly which sign changed in your patient and why does that change affect both variables later?
How does substituting both values into both original equations catch a sign-flip error that slipped through during elimination?
What quick habit can you use when adding/subtracting equations to prevent dropping or flipping a sign?

incorrectWorkedExamples.incorrectWork:
Student work:
1) 2x + 3y = 7
2) 4x − 3y = 1
3) Add the equations: (2x + 4x) + (3y + 3y) = 7 + 1  ← ERROR: changed −3y to +3y when adding
4) 6x + 6y = 8
5) Divide by 6: x + y = 4/3
6) Solve for y: y = 4/3 − x
7) Substitute into 2x + 3y = 7: 2x + 3(4/3 − x) = 7
8) 2x + 4 − 3x = 7
9) −x + 4 = 7
10) −x = 3 ⇒ x = −3
11) y = 4/3 − (−3) = 4/3 + 3 = 13/3
Final: x = −3, y = 13/3

Student work:
1) From 2x + 3y = 7, solve for x: x = (7 − 3y)/2
2) Substitute into 4x − 3y = 1: 4*(7 − 3y)/2 − 3y = 1
3) Simplify the multiplication: 4*(7 − 3y)/2 = 14 + 6y  ← ERROR: sign on −3y flipped when simplifying
4) So 14 + 6y − 3y = 1
5) 14 + 3y = 1
6) 3y = −13 ⇒ y = −13/3
7) Substitute back: x = (7 − 3(−13/3))/2 = (7 + 13)/2 = 20/2 = 10
Final: x = 10, y = −13/3

Student work:
1) 2x + 3y = 7
2) 4x − 3y = 1
3) Subtract second from first: (2x + 3y) − (4x − 3y) = 7 − 1
4) Student expands: 2x + 3y − 4x − 3y = 6  ← ERROR: did not change the sign of −3y when distributing the minus
5) Simplify: −2x + 0 = 6
6) −2x = 6 ⇒ x = −3
7) Substitute into 2x + 3y = 7: 2(−3) + 3y = 7 ⇒ −6 + 3y = 7
8) 3y = 13 ⇒ y = 13/3
Final: x = −3, y = 13/3

required: Projected system 2x + 3y = 7 and 4x − 3y = 1
Printed or projected copies of three incorrect worked examples
Two colored pens or highlighters for groups
optional: Laminated patient cards so groups can write with dry-erase markers

groups.name: Group A: Elimination sign-flip
groups.description: Students who made sign errors while adding or subtracting whole equations. Focus on marking the combined-line error and repairing the combination step.

groups.name: Group B: Substitution sign-flip
groups.description: Students who made sign errors while substituting or distributing across parentheses. Focus on inspecting distribution and fixing the sign during simplification.

studentGroupings.aiRecommendation: Split the class by the error type shown in their previous work: give those who flipped signs during elimination to Group A, and those who flipped signs during substitution to Group B. Keep groups roughly equal in size and mix stronger procedural students across both groups so each group can finish the recompute and verification step.

gapGroups.title: Slope Misidentification in Inequalities
gapGroups.misconceptionSummary: Students identify slope and intercept incorrectly when reading equations of lines, then misapply those values to graphing or shading. This leads to reversed slope/intercept and incorrect half-plane selection.
gapGroups.aiReasoning: PPQ Q5 had 57.1% correct with highConfWrongPct = 0.40, a strong signal that many students hold an incorrect mental model about which number is slope versus intercept. High confident wrong responses elevate conceptual severity toward a structural misunderstanding.
successIndicators: Identify slope and intercept from y = mx + b
Plot line using slope rise/run from intercept
Describe slope as rate of change verbally

targetObjective.description: Students identify slope and intercept incorrectly when reading equations of lines, then misapply those values to graphing or shading. This leads to reversed slope/intercept and incorrect half-plane selection.

prerequisiteGaps.description: Use variables to represent quantities in a real-world or mathematical problem, and construct simple equations and inequalities to solve problems by reasoning about the quantities.

impactedObjectives.description: Represent constraints by equations or inequalities, and by systems of equations and/or inequalities, and interpret solutions as viable or nonviable options in a modeling context. *For example, represent inequalities describing nutritional and cost constraints on combinations of different foods.*

evidence.mostCommonError: Reading b as slope or swapping m and b
sampleStudentWork: Given y = −2x + 3, labeled slope = 3 and intercept = −2
Plotted line with wrong rise/run from intercept
evidence.aiThinkingPattern: Over-relies on number order or signage rather than structure y = mx + b; confuses coefficient role with constant term.

wrongAnswerExplanations.explanation: The student likely misidentified the y-intercept as the slope, confusing the values.
The student may have incorrectly calculated the slope by misapplying the rise/run concept.
The student might have confused the operations needed to find the slope from the equation.

correctAnswerSolution: Identify the equation of the line in slope-intercept form: y = mx + b.
Extract the slope (m) and y-intercept (b) from the equation.
Plot the y-intercept on the graph and use the slope to find another point.
Shade the appropriate half-plane based on the inequality direction.

moveOptions.title: Multiple Representations: Trace the Swap
moveOptions.summary: Students analyze three incorrect student solutions that swap slope and y-intercept, then reconstruct correct graphs using equation, table, and verbal descriptions. The activity surfaces the swap error and connects correct identification of m and b to graphing inequalities as half-planes.
moveOptions.targets: Identify slope and y-intercept from y = mx + b; plot the boundary line using intercept and slope; choose the correct half-plane for a linear inequality
moveOptions.instructionalMove: Display three incorrect student solutions from the "Multiple Entry Points / Representations" structure. Have students mark exactly where the student swapped m and b on each example and justify why that step is wrong. Model one correct reading of y = mx + b, then guide students to build a table and a quick verbal rule to confirm the slope and intercept before plotting. End by connecting the correct boundary to the half-plane test.
moveOptions.aiReasoning: Swapping slope and intercept is a structural misunderstanding of the equation form y = mx + b. Using multiple representations (equation form, a table of values, and a quick verbal rate-of-change statement) makes the roles of m and b visible and reduces reliance on number order. Error-first work on real student solutions forces students to confront the exact swap, then reconstructing the correct representations builds a robust corrective pathway that ties directly to graphing half-planes for inequalities.

whatStudentsDo.label: Examine
whatStudentsDo.detail: Look at three student works that swapped slope and intercept. Mark the exact line where the student writes slope = b and intercept = m.

whatStudentsDo.label: Compare
whatStudentsDo.detail: Make a quick table of x and y values using both the incorrect and correct m/b. Point to how the points move differently.

whatStudentsDo.label: Decide
whatStudentsDo.detail: Use a test point and the original inequality to pick which half-plane to shade after you see the correct boundary.

whatYouDo.label: Display
whatYouDo.detail: Show the three incorrect worked examples on the board or slide. Tell students you want to find the single conceptual mistake in each.

whatYouDo.label: Prompt
whatYouDo.detail: Ask students to mark where m and b are misread. Then ask for a volunteer to build a two-row table to check the slope and intercept.

whatYouDo.label: Model
whatYouDo.detail: Write the correct m and b, draw the correct boundary, and show the half-plane test with (0,0). Repeat the verbal rule: 'm is rise/run; b is where the line crosses y-axis.'

overview.importance: The activity forces students to see the exact swap mistake across equation, table, graph, and words. Making the equation's structure visible across representations helps students correct the root error and preserves ability to graph inequalities correctly later.

setup: Write three inequality prompts and the three incorrect student solutions on the board or a slide. Number them 1–3.
Draw a blank coordinate plane to the right of the examples and have a marker ready.
Have students hold mini-whiteboards or be ready to show thumbs up/down for quick checks.
activitySteps.problem: For each inequality shown, identify the slope and y-intercept, plot the boundary line, and shade the solution half-plane.
coreActivity: Show incorrect example 1. Ask students to circle the step where slope and intercept are named incorrectly. Wait 10 seconds for whiteboard responses.
Ask a volunteer to write a two-row table of x = 0 and x = 1, using the incorrect m/b and then the correct m/b. Point to how y changes between x = 0 and x = 1 in each table.
Repeat for examples 2 and 3. For each, ask: 'Which number is the rise? Which is the intercept?'.
Model the correct graph for one example. Write the verbal rule aloud: 'm is the number multiplied by x — that's the rise/run. b is the y-value at x = 0.'
Have students check the half-plane by plugging (0,0) into the original inequality and then compare that algebraic test to the shaded side on the correct drawing.
Close by asking each student to write one short sentence on their whiteboard: 'To find m and b, I will ...' and hold it up.
discussionQuestions: How did the table of x = 0 and x = 1 make the swap error obvious?
When you swap m and b, what changes about how the line moves on the graph? Use rise/run language.
How does correctly identifying m and b help you choose the half-plane for an inequality?

incorrectWorkedExamples.incorrectWork:
Student work:
1. y ≤ 2x + 3
2. Slope = 3, y-intercept = 2  ← swaps m and b
3. Plot y-intercept at (0, 2).
4. Use slope rise 3 and run 1. Next point is (1, 5).
5. Draw a solid line through (0, 2) and (1, 5).
6. Test point (0, 0): 0 ≤ 2·0 + 3 → 0 ≤ 3 is true, so shade the region containing (0, 0).
Final graph: line through (0, 2) with slope 3 and shaded region containing the origin.

incorrectWorkedExamples.problem: Graph the solution to y > −1/2 x + 4.
incorrectWorkedExamples.incorrectWork:
Student work:
1. y > −1/2 x + 4
2. Slope = 4, y-intercept = −1/2  ← swaps m and b
3. Plot y-intercept at (0, −1/2).
4. Use slope rise 4 and run 1. Next point is (1, 3.5).
5. Draw a dashed line through (0, −1/2) and (1, 3.5).
6. Test point (0, 0): 0 > −1/2·0 + 4 → 0 > 4 is false, so shade the side not containing (0, 0).
Final graph: dashed line through (0, −1/2) with slope 4 and shaded opposite the origin.

incorrectWorkedExamples.problem: Graph the solution to y ≥ 1/2 x − 2.
incorrectWorkedExamples.incorrectWork:
Student work:
1. y ≥ 1/2 x − 2
2. Slope = −2, y-intercept = 1/2  ← swaps m and b
3. Plot y-intercept at (0, 1/2).
4. Use slope rise −2 and run 1. Next point is (1, −3/2).
5. Draw a solid line through (0, 1/2) and (1, −3/2).
6. Test point (0, 0): 0 ≥ 1/2·0 − 2 → 0 ≥ −2 is true, so shade the region containing (0, 0).
Final graph: line through (0, 1/2) with slope −2 and shaded region containing the origin.

required: Board or slide with three incorrect worked examples ready to display
Blank coordinate plane drawn on board or slide
Mini-whiteboards or paper for quick student responses
optional: Printed copies of the three incorrect examples for students
Colored markers to show incorrect vs correct graphs

groups.name: Group A: Needs Concrete Visuals
groups.description: Students who repeatedly swap m and b. Focus on using the table of values and graphing points from x = 0 and x = 1 to see rise/run visually.

groups.name: Group B: Form Recognizers
groups.description: Students who can name m and b sometimes but rush. Focus on verbal rule practice and quick table checks before plotting.

groups.name: Group C: Near-Proficient
groups.description: Students who usually identify m and b correctly but make careless errors. Ask them to lead the table-building step and explain reasoning aloud.

studentGroupings.aiRecommendation: Group by quick whiteboard check at start: have students show slope and intercept on mini-boards. Those who mislabel both go to Group A; those who mislabel occasionally go to Group B; correct labelers go to Group C. Keep whole-class instruction consistent and use groups only for brief targeted prompts or follow-up practice.

moveOptions.title: Construct the Correct Pathway
moveOptions.summary: Students diagnose a student solution that swapped m and b, then build a clear step-by-step pathway from equation to final shaded half-plane. The activity forces error analysis first, then requires students to produce the correct procedure.
moveOptions.targets: Identify slope and y-intercept from y = mx + b and use them to graph the boundary line and shade the correct half-plane for a linear inequality
moveOptions.instructionalMove: Guide students through the Strategy Construction / Corrective Pathway. Give each group a different incorrect student work that swaps m and b and prompt them to mark the single wrong step. Ask each group to write a corrected, numbered pathway from reading the equation to plotting the line and shading the half-plane.
moveOptions.aiReasoning: Students confuse coefficient and constant because they read numbers in order rather than by structure y = mx + b. The Strategy Construction / Corrective Pathway forces learners to turn a diagnosed conceptual mistake into an explicit, correct sequence of steps. Splitting the class for parallel analysis gives each group focused practice finding the single swap error, then requires them to reconstruct the correct pathway, which strengthens the link between symbol structure and graphing procedures needed for later constraint modeling.

whatStudentsDo.label: Analyze
whatStudentsDo.detail: Examine an assigned incorrect solution and underline the one step where m and b were swapped.

whatStudentsDo.label: Construct
whatStudentsDo.detail: Write a numbered pathway that starts with reading y = mx + b and ends with a plotted boundary and correct half-plane.

whatStudentsDo.label: Present
whatStudentsDo.detail: Share the repaired pathway with the class and point out how it prevents the swap error.

whatYouDo.label: Assign
whatYouDo.detail: Give each group a different incorrect worked example that shows the swap error.

whatYouDo.label: Circulate
whatYouDo.detail: Move between the two groups, asking quick diagnostic questions and nudging toward a clear first step: 'What is m? What is b?'

whatYouDo.label: Highlight
whatYouDo.detail: After presentations, write one teacher-crafted pathway that combines correct wording and visual cues.

overview.importance: The activity targets the exact swap error by forcing students to name the single wrong step and then produce a faultless procedure. Building the pathway makes the structure of y = mx + b visible and links it to plotting and shading.

setup: Write the central problem on the board: Graph the solution set for y ≤ −1/2 x + 2.
Prepare three printed or projected incorrect worked examples showing only the m↔b swap error.
Split the class into two groups: Group A examines Example 1, Group B examines Example 2. Tell students Example 3 will be discussed whole-class after presentations.
activitySteps.problem: Graph the solution set for y ≤ -1/2x + 2. Show your numbered pathway from reading the equation to plotting the boundary and shading the half-plane.
coreActivity: Tell Group A to analyze Example 1 and Group B to analyze Example 2 for 8 minutes.
Ask each group to mark the single incorrect step and write one sentence naming the misconception (swap m and b).
Have each group build a numbered, step-by-step corrective pathway that starts '1. Read y = mx + b: m = __, b = __.' and ends with shading the correct half-plane.
Bring the class back. Ask one member from each group to present their corrected pathway in two minutes.
Show Example 3 to the whole class. Ask volunteers to explain why the swap would change the plotted boundary and check whether the swap would change the shading.
Write a teacher model pathway on the board that students copy into their notes.
discussionQuestions: Exactly which line in the student work shows the swap, and how would you write that line correctly?
How does swapping m and b change the rise/run you use from the intercept?
Which step tells you how to decide which half-plane to shade and why does identifying m and b correctly matter for that step?

incorrectWorkedExamples.problem: Problem: For y = 3x + 5, identify the slope and y-intercept and sketch the line.
incorrectWorkedExamples.incorrectWork:
y = 3x + 5
Slope = 5, y-intercept = 3  ← swapped m and b
Plot the y-intercept at (0, 3).
Use slope: rise 5, run 1. From (0, 3) go to (1, 8).
Draw the line through (0, 3) and (1, 8).

incorrectWorkedExamples.problem: Problem: For y = −2x − 4, identify the slope and y-intercept and sketch the line.
incorrectWorkedExamples.incorrectWork:
y = −2x − 4
Slope = −4, y-intercept = −2  ← swapped m and b
Plot the y-intercept at (0, −2).
Use slope: rise −4, run 1. From (0, −2) go to (1, −6).
Draw the line through (0, −2) and (1, −6).

incorrectWorkedExamples.problem: Problem: For y > 1/2 x + 1, identify the slope and y-intercept and sketch the boundary line.
incorrectWorkedExamples.incorrectWork:
y > 1/2 x + 1
Slope = 1, y-intercept = 1/2  ← swapped m and b
Plot the y-intercept at (0, 1/2).
Use slope: rise 1, run 1. From (0, 1/2) go to (1, 1.5).
Since inequality is >, draw a dotted line along those points and indicate shading above that line.

required: Projected slide or printout with the three incorrect worked examples
Board or large paper to write the teacher model pathway
Graph paper or coordinate grid on board
optional: Sticky notes for groups to post their numbered pathway

groups.name: Group A: Swapped m and b
groups.description: Students who wrote the slope and intercept reversed on their exit ticket. Focus: diagnose the swap and construct a correct read-and-plot pathway.

groups.name: Group B: Plotting error after identification
groups.description: Students who identified m and b but misapplied rise/run when plotting. Focus: translate m into rise/run from the intercept and confirm half-plane.

studentGroupings.aiRecommendation: Group students based on their exit-ticket pattern: those who swapped m and b versus those who misapplied slope when plotting. Spend more time with Group A early, then rotate quickly to check Group B's constructed pathway.

gapGroups.title: Boundary Line Style Confusion
gapGroups.misconceptionSummary: Students choose solid versus dashed boundary lines incorrectly and sometimes shade the wrong half-plane. The graph either includes the boundary for a strict inequality or excludes it for a nonstrict inequality.
gapGroups.aiReasoning: PPQ Q6 accuracy about 53.6% with moderate confidence differences indicates many students can perform graphing steps but misapply the inclusion rule for boundary lines. The error appears procedural but impacts correctness of solution sets.
successIndicators: Choose dashed line for strict inequalities
Shade the correct half-plane after testing a point
Annotate boundary inclusion or exclusion

targetObjective.description: Students choose solid versus dashed boundary lines incorrectly and sometimes shade the wrong half-plane. The graph either includes the boundary for a strict inequality or excludes it for a nonstrict inequality.

prerequisiteGaps.description: Use variables to represent quantities in a real-world or mathematical problem, and construct simple equations and inequalities to solve problems by reasoning about the quantities.

impactedObjectives.description: Represent constraints by equations or inequalities, and by systems of equations and/or inequalities, and interpret solutions as viable or nonviable options in a modeling context. *For example, represent inequalities describing nutritional and cost constraints on combinations of different foods.*

evidence.mostCommonError: Using solid line for > or < and not testing a point
sampleStudentWork: Graphed y ≥ 2x + 1 with dashed line
Shaded wrong side of line without test point
evidence.aiThinkingPattern: Confuses inclusive notation with line style and skips point-testing strategy; rule-application error rather than arithmetic failure.

wrongAnswerExplanations.explanation: The student may have confused the inequality type, thinking it was nonstrict and thus used a solid line when it should have been dashed.
The student likely misinterpreted the inequality direction, shading the wrong half-plane.
The student may have incorrectly applied the boundary line style, possibly thinking both sides of the inequality should be included.
The student might have mixed up both the boundary line style and the shading direction, leading to confusion in the representation of the inequality.

correctAnswerSolution: Identify the inequality type: strict (>) or nonstrict (≥).
Determine the boundary line style: solid for nonstrict, dashed for strict.
Shade the appropriate half-plane based on the inequality direction.

moveOptions.title: Unpack a Favorite No
moveOptions.summary: Students analyze an anonymous incorrect graphing solution (a "Favorite No") to identify the single error about line style or shading and then correct the graph. The activity forces students to confront the specific rule confusion and practice the point-test method.
moveOptions.targets: Choosing dashed vs solid boundary lines and shading the correct half-plane after testing a point
moveOptions.instructionalMove: Guide the whole class through a Favorite No review. Show each anonymous incorrect student solution and ask the class to locate the single error (line style or half-plane choice). Model a short point-test and a clear annotation that marks boundary inclusion or exclusion. Have students propose the corrected graph and a one-sentence reason.
moveOptions.aiReasoning: The Favorite No structure centers on a real student error so teachers can target the exact rule confusion: line style versus inequality strictness and failure to point-test. Having students diagnose a single error encourages attention to the conceptual rule instead of procedural guessing. Guided analysis plus immediate correction builds the point-test habit and links graph choices to algebraic form, preparing students for later constraint modeling with systems of inequalities.

whatStudentsDo.label: Analyze
whatStudentsDo.detail: Read one anonymous student solution on the board and find the single incorrect step about the boundary line or shading.

whatStudentsDo.label: Explain
whatStudentsDo.detail: Say which inequality symbol means the boundary is included and why the drawn line is correct or incorrect.

whatStudentsDo.label: Correct
whatStudentsDo.detail: Propose and justify a fixed graph using a quick point-test and annotate the boundary as included or excluded.

whatYouDo.label: Display
whatYouDo.detail: Project or write each Favorite No on the board with a clear coordinate grid and the student's steps visible.

whatYouDo.label: Prompt
whatYouDo.detail: Ask students to find the single error and guide a short whole-class discussion to confirm the error and correction.

whatYouDo.label: Model
whatYouDo.detail: Demonstrate the point-test and label the boundary with 'include' or 'exclude' and a quick justification sentence.

overview.importance: This activity targets the precise rule confusion: students misapply solid/dashed conventions and skip point-testing. Diagnosing one anonymous error surfaces the thinking behind the mistake and gives a concrete corrective routine to use on future graphs.

setup: Draw a blank coordinate plane on the board or project one.
Write or project three anonymous student solutions (Favorite Nos) that each contain exactly one boundary/half-plane error.
Prepare quick student response tools: mini whiteboards or a thumbs-up/ thumbs-down signal.
activitySteps.problem: For each Favorite No, identify the one incorrect decision (boundary style or shaded half-plane). Explain why it is wrong. Show the corrected graph and write one sentence that justifies the correction using a point test and an 'include' or 'exclude' label for the boundary.
coreActivity: Project the first Favorite No and read it aloud.
Ask: 'Where is the single wrong decision? Line style or shaded side?' Wait 10 seconds for silent thumbs or whiteboard answers.
Call on 2 students to justify their choice in one sentence. If they disagree, ask them to point to the step and read it.
Model a quick point-test on the board using (0,0) or an off-line point. Write the inequality check and label 'include' or 'exclude' on the boundary.
Have the class agree on the corrected graph. Repeat for the other two Favorite Nos.
discussionQuestions: How does the inequality symbol tell you whether the boundary should be solid or dashed?
Why does testing one point guarantee you shaded the correct half-plane?
When you convert 2x − 3y ≥ 6 to y ≤ (2/3)x − 2, which two steps must you check before drawing the boundary?

incorrectWorkedExamples.incorrectWork:
Step 1: Put the inequality in slope-intercept form. y < 2x + 1.
Step 2: Identify slope = 2 and y-intercept = 1. Plot (0, 1) and use slope to draw the line.
Step 3: Because < is not inclusive, draw a solid line through the points.  ← single error: used solid line for a strict inequality
Step 4: Test the point (0, 0): 0 < 2(0) + 1 → 0 < 1 is true. Shade the side containing (0, 0).
Final: Solid line through (0,1) with the half-plane containing (0,0) shaded. (Boundary is included.)

Step 1: Put the inequality in slope-intercept form. y > −x + 3.
Step 2: Identify slope = −1 and y-intercept = 3. Plot (0, 3) and use slope to draw a dashed line.  ← dashed line is correct
Step 3: Do not test any point. Decide to shade the side below the line because it looks like the smaller y-values.  ← single error: shaded wrong half-plane without a point test
Step 4: Shade the region below the dashed line.
Final: Dashed line through (0,3) with the lower half-plane shaded. (Wrong half-plane.)

Step 1: Solve for y. 2x − 3y ≥ 6.
Step 2: Rearrange: −3y ≥ 6 − 2x.
Step 3: Multiply by −1 and flip inequality: 3y ≤ −6 + 2x so y ≤ (2/3)x − 2.
Step 4: Since the algebra gives ≤, draw a dashed line for the boundary.  ← single error: used dashed line instead of solid for a nonstrict inequality
Step 5: Test (0,0): 0 ≤ (2/3)(0) − 2 → 0 ≤ −2 is false, so shade the other side.
Final: Dashed boundary for y ≤ (2/3)x − 2 with the correct half-plane shaded. (Boundary incorrectly excluded.)

required: Board or projector with a coordinate plane
Three Favorite No slides or written examples ready to display
optional: Student mini whiteboards or paper for a 10-second response
Colored markers to show 'include' vs 'exclude' visually

groups.name: Group A: Needs Concrete Support
groups.description: Students who draw the wrong line style or cannot perform a point-test. Focus: practice identifying ≤/≥ versus </> and one-step point tests.

groups.name: Group B: Partial Understanding
groups.description: Students who know the line style but sometimes pick the wrong half-plane. Focus: reinforce point-testing and annotating boundary inclusion.

groups.name: Group C: Ready to Extend
groups.description: Students who consistently draw correct graphs but skip annotation. Focus: practice explaining why a point satisfies or fails the inequality and linking to systems of inequalities.

studentGroupings.aiRecommendation: Form groups quickly based on an exit-ticket or a one-question pre-check: ask 'Is y ≤ or <—solid or dashed?' Use same-group partners for a single turn-and-talk after each Favorite No. Keep whole-class discussion dominant.

moveOptions.title: Boundary Line Diagnosis
moveOptions.summary: Students analyze three incorrect student graphs that each show the boundary-style or shading error. The task forces identification of the single conceptual mistake and requires a corrected graph with a point-test annotation.
moveOptions.targets: Choosing dashed vs solid boundary lines correctly and shading the correct half-plane after performing a point-test
moveOptions.instructionalMove: Guide two small groups through an Incorrect Worked Example Analysis of real student work that shows either using a solid line for a strict inequality or shading the wrong half-plane. Tell each group to find the single error, run a point-test, and produce a corrected graph with an annotation that states whether the boundary is included. Visit each group briefly to prompt the point-test and to ask them to justify why the original step is incorrect.
moveOptions.aiReasoning: Students confuse the rule for boundary style and skip point-testing. An Incorrect Worked Example Analysis focuses attention on the single conceptual slip by asking students to diagnose and repair a real error. Splitting the class lets two groups analyze different examples in parallel, producing immediate comparisons that reinforce the rule about dashed vs solid lines and the necessity of a point-test. This structure directly targets the rule-application error and surfaces prerequisite steps like isolating y to identify the boundary and testing a point to pick the half-plane.

whatStudentsDo.label: **Analyze**
whatStudentsDo.detail: Work in a small group to read one incorrect student solution step-by-step and find the single mistake about line style or shading.

whatStudentsDo.label: **Test**
whatStudentsDo.detail: Choose a test point and substitute it into the inequality to confirm which half-plane satisfies the inequality.

whatStudentsDo.label: **Correct**
whatStudentsDo.detail: Draw the corrected boundary line (solid or dashed), shade the correct half-plane, and write a one-sentence annotation explaining the inclusion or exclusion of the boundary.

whatYouDo.label: **Assign**
whatYouDo.detail: Split the class into two groups and give each group a different incorrect worked example to analyze.

whatYouDo.label: **Circulate**
whatYouDo.detail: Visit each group for 3–4 minutes. Ask them to show the test-point check and to point to the single error.

whatYouDo.label: **Debrief**
whatYouDo.detail: Bring class together to compare the two corrected graphs and highlight why the original step caused the incorrect final graph.

overview.importance: The exercise isolates the single rule confusion (line style vs inequality sign) and builds the habit of a test-point check. By correcting a real error, students link symbolic rules to a visible graph and to future modeling tasks that depend on correct constraint representation.

setup: Prepare three short incorrect worked examples on slide or chart paper. Number them 1–3.
Have two large blank coordinate grids on the board or two erasable whiteboards ready for group corrections.
Split the class into two groups by the common error: Group A will analyze example 1, Group B will analyze example 2. Example 3 is a quick whole-class compare during the debrief.
activitySteps.problem: For your assigned incorrect worked example, identify the single error (line style or shading), run a point-test to justify the correct half-plane, redraw the corrected graph with the proper boundary style, and write a one-sentence annotation explaining whether the boundary is included or excluded.
coreActivity: Step 1: Tell Group A to analyze Incorrect Worked Example 1 and Group B to analyze Incorrect Worked Example 2. Give each group a whiteboard or paper.
Step 2: Ask groups to read the student work aloud and underline the single step they think is wrong. Write that step in one sentence.
Step 3: Require a point-test. Have each group pick (0, 0) or another easy point, substitute it into the inequality, and record true/false and the implication for shading.
Step 4: Have each group redraw the graph with the correct boundary style and shading. Add a one-sentence annotation: 'Boundary is included/excluded because...' and show the test calculation.
Step 5: Reconvene whole class. Display both corrected graphs plus Incorrect Worked Example 3. Ask groups to compare decisions and point to exactly which step was fixed.
Step 6: Have volunteers present the corrected annotation and explain why the original produced the wrong final graph.
discussionQuestions: Which single step changed the final graph the most: switching the boundary style or changing the shaded half-plane? Explain why.
How does a point-test guarantee you chose the correct half-plane when the boundary style is clear?
If you isolated y before graphing, where would a mistake in algebraic manipulation affect your line and shading choices?

incorrectWorkedExamples.incorrectWork:
Step 1: Replace inequality with boundary: y = 2x + 1.
Step 2: Since the inequality is >, draw a solid line.  ← (error: should be dashed)
Step 3: Test point (0, 0): 0 > 2(0) + 1 → 0 > 1 is false. So shade the half-plane that does not contain (0, 0).
Final graph: Solid line through y = 2x + 1 with shading above the line (the side not containing (0, 0)).

Step 1: Replace inequality with boundary: y = −x + 4.
Step 2: Since the inequality is ≤, draw a dashed line.  ← (error: should be solid)
Step 3: Test point (0, 0): 0 ≤ −0 + 4 → 0 ≤ 4 is true. So shade the half-plane that contains (0, 0).
Final graph: Dashed line through y = −x + 4 with shading on the side containing (0, 0).

Step 1: Replace inequality with boundary: y = -3x + 2.
Step 2: Since the inequality is >, draw a solid line.  ← (error: should be dashed)
Step 3: Test point (0, 0): 0 > -3(0) + 2 → 0 > 2 is false. So shade the half-plane that does not contain (0, 0).
Final graph: Solid line through y = -3x + 2 with shading above the line (the half-plane not containing (0, 0)).

required: Three prepared incorrect examples on a slide or chart paper
Two small whiteboards or erasable boards for group corrections
Markers and an eraser
optional: Graph paper handouts
Colored markers (one color for boundary, one for shading)

groups.name: Group A: Solid-line for '>' error
groups.description: Students who used a solid line for a strict inequality. Focus on identifying why the boundary should be dashed and how that changes the graph.

groups.name: Group B: Dashed-line or wrong-shading error
groups.description: Students who used a dashed line incorrectly for ≤ or chose the wrong half-plane. Focus on point-testing and linking test results to shading.

studentGroupings.aiRecommendation: Group students by the specific error they made on the exit ticket: those who used solid lines for '>' go to Group A; those who used dashed lines where ≤ was required or shaded the wrong side go to Group B. Keep groups whole for the activity; rotate between groups to prompt the point-test and the one-sentence annotation.

savedNextSteps.misconceptionSummary: Students compute or read the slope incorrectly when forming or graphing inequalities, causing wrong shading or boundary orientation. The result is incorrect half-planes or reversed solution sets on graph-based inequality tasks.
successIndicators: Explain slope sign and shading direction aloud
Plot line with correct slope from two points
Shade the half-plane consistent with the inequality
savedNextSteps.activitySummary: Students analyze three incorrect worked examples that show slope-reading and shading errors, then fix the mistakes and produce a correct graph. The activity forces attention to how slope sign and inequality direction determine line orientation and which half-plane to shade.
savedNextSteps.aiReasoning: Making the algebraic structure explicit (m and b roles, sign of m, inequality direction) targets the root cause: students do not map equation features to geometric orientation. Having students inspect incorrect examples forces them to locate the single conceptual slip (misreading sign or swapping values) and then reapply the structure. The small-group, error-analysis format connects the fix to prior gaps (rewriting standard form, solving for y) and shows how the same error would break later modeling tasks like representing constraints with inequalities.
